info.name: Auth – Change Password (POST /api/v1/auth/change-password)
info.description:
E2E tests for POST /api/v1/auth/change-password (requires JWT).

Folder execution order (orchestrated by make e2e-change-password):
  1. setup             — register gmail_test_email, fetch verification OTP from Gmail, verify email
  2. happy-path        — login (capture pre-change access token + refresh cookie) →
                         correct old_password + valid new_password → 200,
                         refresh cookie cleared in response (MaxAge negative),
                         GET /me with pre-change access token → 401 (JTI blocklisted),
                         login with old password → 401, login with new password → 200
  3. failures          — wrong old_password → 401, same password → 422 (ErrSamePassword),
                         5 consecutive wrong attempts → 429 too_many_attempts,
                         reset counter via correct change, subsequent wrong attempt → 401 not 429,
                         new_password too short → 422, missing old_password → 422,
                         missing new_password → 422
  4. auth-failures     — missing Authorization header → 401, tampered token signature → 401
  5. validation        — body > 1 MiB → 413, malformed JSON → 422
  6. rate-limiting-cpw — cpw:ip: bucket (burst=5): 5 warmups → 6th → 429

All test users use @e2e.test emails (or gmail_test_email) so `make _e2e-db-clean` can wipe them.
Run `make _e2e-db-clean` and `make _e2e-kv-clean` before this collection.

Notes on what is NOT covered here and why:
  - refresh cookie cleared at MaxAge derived from expires_at: handler_test.go covers cookie attrs.
  - Change-password revokes ALL sessions (two-device): requires raw HttpOnly cookie access across
    two concurrent Postman sessions; covered by store_test.go TestUpdatePasswordHashTx.
  - Failed-attempt audit row written: requires a DB query step; covered by
    service_test.go TestService_UpdatePasswordHash/wrong_old_password_writes_audit_and_returns_ErrInvalidCredentials.
  - Blocklist failure → 200: non-fatal; covered by handler_test.go.
  - Individual password-strength rule variants (no-upper, no-lower, no-digit, no-symbol, >72 bytes):
    covered by handler_test.go + service_test.go; one representative E2E case (too short) confirms wiring.

exec: // Increment the request sequence counter and derive a unique X-Forwarded-For IP
// for every request so non-RL folders never exhaust any rate-limit bucket.
// The rate-limiting folder overrides _xff to 127.0.0.1 via its own prerequest.

item.name: setup
item.description: Registers the gmail_test_email user, fetches the verification OTP from Gmail, and verifies the email. All subsequent folders depend on cpw_email being verified in the DB.

exec: console.error('[ERROR] gmail_test_email not set in environment — add it to environment.json.');
if (!token) { console.warn('[WARN] gmail_access_token not set — skipping Gmail fetch'); return; }
console.log('[INFO] Waiting 8 s for verification email to arrive in Gmail...');
console.log('[INFO] Done waiting. Querying Gmail API...');
if (err) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail list failed:', err); return; }
if (res.code === 401) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail token expired — refresh at https://developers.google.com/oauthplayground'); return; }
if (!msgs || !msgs.length) { console.warn('[WARN] No matching verification email found in Gmail.'); return; }
if (err2) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail fetch failed:', err2); return; }
const p = parts.find(x => x.mimeType === 'text/plain') || parts.find(x => x.mimeType === 'text/html');
if (!body) { console.warn('[WARN] Could not decode verification email body.'); return; }
if (!match) { console.warn('[WARN] No 6-digit OTP found in verification email.'); return; }
console.log('[INFO] Verification OTP stored in cpw_vfy_code:', match[1]);
console.error('[ERROR] cpw_vfy_code is empty — Gmail fetch in register step did not complete.');
console.error('        Check: gmail_access_token is set, token not expired, --delay-request >= 8000.');

item.name: happy-path
item.description:
Full change-password scenario in deterministic order:
  1.  Login → capture pre-change access token (cpw_pre_change_token)
  2.  POST /change-password correct old+new → 200, refresh cookie cleared
  3.  GET /me with cpw_pre_change_token → 401 (JTI blocklisted)
  4.  POST /login with OLD password → 401 (confirms old password rejected)
  5.  POST /login with NEW password → 200 (confirms new password hash persisted)

exec: // Stash this token BEFORE the change so we can later verify it is JTI-blocklisted.
console.log('[INFO] Pre-change access token saved as cpw_pre_change_token.');
// Correct old_password + a strong new_password that differs from the current one.
// UpdatePasswordHashTx commits: password hash updated, all active sessions revoked,
// refresh cookie cleared in response, current access token JTI-blocklisted.
// The refresh_token cookie must be cleared (negative Max-Age).
// Handler calls token.ClearRefreshCookie which sets Max-Age to -1.
// Some proxies strip Set-Cookie when no value is set; tolerate absence.
console.warn('[WARN] Max-Age directive not found in Set-Cookie header.');

item.name: GET /me with PRE-CHANGE access token → 401 (JTI blocklisted)

exec: // The handler writes the token's JTI to the KV store (BlockToken) after a
// successful change. The auth middleware checks the JTI blocklist and rejects
// the request even though the token signature is still valid.
// This confirms the KV blocklist integration is wired end-to-end.

item.name: POST /login with OLD password → 401 (confirms old password rejected after change)

exec: // The old password must no longer work after the change.

item.name: POST /login with NEW password → 200 (confirms new password hash persisted in DB)

exec: // A 200 confirms the new password hash is persisted in the DB.
// Stash this token for the subsequent failure tests that need a valid JWT.

item.name: failures
item.description:
Credential-failure, same-password, wrong-attempt threshold, and strength-failure paths.
All tests in this folder use cpw_access_token (new password obtained at end of happy-path).
Tests are ordered so that the consecutive-wrong-attempt counter is exercised in a controlled way:
  1–5: obtain a fresh token, then make wrong-password attempts to hit the 429 threshold.
  6:   correct change resets the counter so subsequent wrong attempts return 401 again.

exec: // Attempt count = 1. Well below the 5-attempt threshold.
// new_password equals the current password (cpw_new_password set in setup).
// UpdatePasswordHash checks for same-password before persisting.
// ErrSamePassword is mapped to validation_error (422) by the handler.

item.name: new_password too short (< 8 chars) → 422 validation_error (representative strength case)

exec: // validateChangePasswordRequest only checks field presence; strength is validated
// This one case confirms the strength-check path is wired end-to-end.

item.name: POST /register → 201 (setup threshold-test user — no email verification needed)

exec: // Register a fresh @e2e.test account for the consecutive-wrong-attempt test.
// No email verification is needed because we log in via the lockout path:
// the attempt counter check fires before the email_verified guard in the
// Actually — change-password requires a valid JWT, so we do need a verified
// account and a real login. We use an @e2e.test address so no Gmail OTP is
// needed; instead we manually seed email_verified=true via direct registration
// followed by the verify-email bypass approach using a fresh timestamp email.
// Since seeding without email is not available here, use the gmail account
// and re-login after the happy-path password change (cpw_new_password is now set).
// This request just registers an extra standalone user for threshold isolation.

item.name: 5 consecutive wrong old_password → 429 too_many_attempts (with forgot-password hint)
item.description: Sends 5 consecutive wrong-old-password requests on the main account (cpw_email / cpw_access_token). After 5 wrong attempts the service increments past the threshold and returns ErrTooManyAttempts. The handler maps this to 429 too_many_attempts with a forgot-password hint in the message body.

exec: // We need a fresh valid access token. cpw_access_token was set at end of
// happy-path (login with cpw_new_password). The previous wrong-attempt in this
// folder used that token but a valid token is still needed (JWT itself is valid).
// wrong attempts 1 (from earlier test) + 4 more here = 5 total → 429 on 5th.
// We fire 4 more wrong-old-password requests inline then assert 429 on this one.
if (!token) { console.error('[ERROR] cpw_access_token is not set — re-run happy-path first.'); return; }
body: { mode: 'raw', raw: JSON.stringify({ old_password: 'Wr0ng!Password', new_password: 'AnotherP@ss3!' }) }
body: { mode: 'raw', raw: JSON.stringify({ old_password: 'Wr0ng!Password', new_password: 'AnotherP@ss3!' }) }
body: { mode: 'raw', raw: JSON.stringify({ old_password: 'Wr0ng!Password', new_password: 'AnotherP@ss3!' }) }
// The response body must contain a hint directing the user to forgot-password.

item.name: POST /login with new password → 200 (re-acquire fresh token after threshold test)
successful change resets counter: subsequent wrong attempt → 401 (not 429)
item.description: Issue a successful change-password to reset the failure counter, then immediately try another wrong old_password. The counter is 0 after the successful change, so the service returns 401 invalid_credentials, not 429 too_many_attempts.

exec: // Perform a successful change-password first to reset the attempt counter.
// cpw_new_password → cpw_reset_password. Store new password for later login.
body: { mode: 'raw', raw: JSON.stringify({ old_password: pm.collectionVariables.get('cpw_new_password'), new_password: 'ResetCpwP@ss3!' }) }
console.warn('[WARN] Counter-reset change-password failed:', err || res.code);
console.log('[INFO] Counter reset via successful change-password.');
// Re-login to get a fresh token with the newest password.
body: { mode: 'raw', raw: JSON.stringify({ identifier: pm.collectionVariables.get('cpw_email'), password: 'ResetCpwP@ss3!' }) }
console.log('[INFO] Fresh token stored in cpw_access_token3.');
console.warn('[WARN] Re-login after counter-reset failed:', err2 || res2.code);
// Counter is now 0. A single wrong attempt must return 401 (not 429).
pm.test('status is 401 (counter reset — wrong attempt returns 401, not 429)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(401));

item.name: auth-failures
item.description:
Verifies that the JWT auth middleware correctly guards POST /change-password.
These tests do not require a specific user in the DB — any request without a valid
Access token must be rejected before the handler body runs.

exec: // No Authorization header at all — the auth middleware must reject this.
// A JWT with the last character of the signature replaced by 'X'.
// The auth middleware validates the signature and must reject this with 401.

item.name: validation
item.description:
Body-size and malformed-JSON cases for POST /change-password.
The rate limiter and auth middleware fire before body decoding, so these requests
use a real (valid) access token from cpw_access_token3 — but any valid token works
because the body is rejected before the service is called.

exec: // Pad the body past the 1 MiB MaxBytesReader limit using a valid JSON field.
// http.MaxBytesReader fires during json.Decoder.Decode → respond.DecodeJSON maps to 413.

item.name: rate-limiting-cpw
item.description:
Verifies the cpw:ip: IP rate limiter for POST /change-password (burst=5, rate=5/15 min).

The folder prerequest overrides _xff to 127.0.0.1 so all requests share one bucket.
Warmup requests use an empty body (→ 422 at handler-level validation) so the service
is never called and no real credentials are required, but the rate-limit middleware
fires first and each request still consumes a slot.

Sequence:
  1–5. warmup 1/5 … 5/5 → not 429 (slots 1–5 consumed)
  6.   6th request → 429 (bucket exhausted)

Covers checklist item: 6th request from same IP within 15 min → 429 (cpw:ip:, burst=5).

exec: // Pin all requests in this folder to 127.0.0.1 so they share one cpw:ip: bucket.

item.name: warmup 5/5 — consume slot (→ not 429, last allowed)

exec: pm.test('not rate-limited yet (cpw:ip: slot 5 — last allowed)', () => pm.expect(pm.response.code).to.not.eql(429));

variable.description: Auto-incremented counter used by the collection prerequest to generate unique X-Forwarded-For IPs.
Current X-Forwarded-For value. Set to a unique IP by the collection prerequest; overridden to 127.0.0.1 by the rate-limiting folder prerequest.
Set from gmail_test_email in setup/register prerequest. The verified account used throughout happy-path and failures folders.
Initial password for cpw_email. Set in setup/register prerequest.
New password used in the successful change-password step in happy-path. Set in setup/register prerequest.
Verification OTP fetched from Gmail by setup/register test script. Consumed by setup/verify-email.
Access token saved from the pre-change login. Used in happy-path/GET /me step to verify the JTI blocklist is active after the change.
Access token obtained after the successful change-password in happy-path (login with cpw_new_password). Used by the failures folder.
Access token obtained after re-login following the threshold-429 test. Used for the counter-reset test.
Access token obtained after the counter-reset change-password + re-login. Used by validation and as the active token for subsequent tests.
Password set during the counter-reset change-password step (ResetCpwP@ss3!). Stored for reference.
Timestamp-unique @e2e.test email registered for the threshold-test isolation setup step.
